---
description: "How to write this repo's canonical one-liner description"
applyTo: "**/package.json, **/Cargo.toml, **/README.md, **/doc.go"
---

# Project Description Guidelines

Every repo has **one canonical description** made of up to two parts:

- **Part 1 — the one-liner.** A single sentence that is reused **verbatim everywhere** the project
  is described by machine: `package.json` `description`, `Cargo.toml` `description` (workspace
  package), the first line of Go `doc.go`, and the first sentence of the `README.md` intro.
- **Part 2 — the elaboration (optional).** One extra sentence that adds the "how / built on / where
  it's used". Part 2 is **maintained only in the `README.md`** (and, for Go, the package `doc.go`,
  which is that project's human surface on pkg.go.dev). It never appears in machine description
  fields.

## The one-liner formula

`[Scope/Runtime] + [Artifact type] + for + [Primary purpose]` — one sentence, no leading verb, no
marketing, ≤ 12 words (hard cap ~15), ends with a period.

- **Scope/Runtime** — include only when distinguishing: `Go`, `Rust framework`,
  `for JavaScript and TypeScript runtimes`, `Server-side`.
- **Artifact type** — the precise noun: `parser`, `client`, `library and CLI`, `framework`,
  `dashboard`, `template`, `IaC`, `codec and state machine`, `database`. Avoid vague `tool` /
  `application`.
- **Primary purpose** — the single most important job. If you need "and" to join a _second_ purpose,
  pick the dominant one.

## Rules

- Lead with the noun, not a verb (`Native rqlite client …`).
- One purpose only.
- Qualifiers must earn their place (`Standalone`, `Native`, `Zero-dependency`,
  `Object-storage-native` each encode a real differentiator).
- **Describe what the repo OWNS, not what it VENDORS.** Synced / generated / vendored code (anything
  with a `// Generated by …` header, `_ui/` dirs synced from `ui`, lifted upstream packages) is an
  implementation detail, not part of the one-liner. Test: "if this synced dir got pulled fresh on
  the next build, would the description still be true?" If yes, leave it out.
  - Exception: when vendored code _is_ the product (e.g. `logql-syntax` lifts upstream Loki packages
    on purpose), name it.
- Self-test: "Could this sentence describe a different project?" (too generic) and "Does it claim
  something the code doesn't do, or hide owned scope?" (inaccurate).

## Where the one-liner is deployed (keep in sync)

| Surface                          | Applies to                          |
| -------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| `package.json` `description`     | JS/TS packages, and cloud/dx/ui/etc |
| `Cargo.toml` workspace `package` | Rust crates                         |
| `README.md` first sentence       | all                                 |

For **Go**, the `README.md` first sentence is canonical. The `doc.go` package comment is the
package's own human surface on pkg.go.dev and uses idiomatic `Package x …` phrasing; keep its first
sentence _consistent in meaning_ with Part 1, but it may be package-scoped (the library) rather than
repo-scoped, and may carry its own elaboration.

`Cargo.toml` per-crate `description` fields describe the **individual crate**, not the project, and
are written independently (see `varv-rs/crates/*`). `wrangler.jsonc` has no description field — only
`name`. GitHub repo "About" text is set manually and should match Part 1.

## Example templates

`*-example` repos are deliberately asymmetric: the machine `description` ships a real value
(`Production-ready template for …`), while the `README.md` keeps a placeholder
`<!-- One-liner: … -->` for downstream users to fill in. Do not fill the template README one-liner.
